title: The status and politics of bicycling in the cities of low- and middle-income countries
data_sources: heterogeneous secondary urban-profile sources (demographics, pollution, road injury, transport)

data_scope.data_availability: tools released as Supplementary Information 1-4; limited anonymized intercept-survey subset on request; ethnographic/interview/full survey data restricted; no code released

core_conclusion: In four LMIC cities (Delhi, Chennai, Dhaka, Accra), everyday bicycling persists but is politically invisible, done mainly by low-income adult men while women and children cycle within neighborhood enclaves. Barriers extend well beyond bike lanes to financial, maintenance, manufacturing/design, and human-social infrastructures. Read through a mobility-justice lens, imported high-income-country infrastructure logics fail these riders; the authors call for an ethics-of-care, repair-oriented approach stewarded by health and environmental agencies.
why_it_matters: Recenters cycling policy on existing marginalized riders rather than future car-converting elites; shows infrastructure is financial/maintenance/social as well as physical; ties active travel to noncommunicable-disease and climate burdens concentrated in LMICs; names a scattered-governance gap and proposes health-agency stewardship.
why_nature: Couples space, society and health in the Global South; offers a transferable critique of uncritical HIC policy transfer; brings a rigorous mixed-methods design complementing the journal's quantitative urban science; highly policy-translatable (ethics of care, repair-oriented governance).
added_value_to_my_research: Georeference invisible cyclists into a spatiotemporal exposure/risk surface (KDE + explainable ML)
Model repair/parking/retail shops as an accessibility supply layer via 2SFCA/R2SFCA
Scenario health-impact model: protect current cyclists vs convert car users (Monte Carlo)
Causal facility-design study of low-stress connected networks via network community detection
Gender/age-specific catchment functions for a behaviorally honest 15-minute proximity metric
